Description:
Implement full observability across the platform using OpenTelemetry distributed tracing, Grafana dashboards, Prometheus and Loki for metrics and log aggregation, Sentry for error tracking, and a dedicated observability agent enforcing schema conformance and hallucination alerting.

SCOPE:
- OpenTelemetry: distributed tracing and metrics across all agents and routers
- Grafana dashboards: latency p50/p95, token usage, hallucination rate, retrieval precision, reranker confidence
- Prometheus + Loki: metrics scraping and structured JSON log aggregation from stdout
- Sentry: error tracking and alerting; Microsoft Clarity for frontend session replay
- Observability agent: traces, schema conformance rate >= 99%, hallucination alert if > 1%

MILESTONE: MVP 3 — Full Twelve-Factor Compliance, Observability & Auth

Instrument all FastAPI routers and agent modules with OpenTelemetry distributed tracing and metrics.

IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS:
- Add opentelemetry-sdk, opentelemetry-instrumentation-fastapi, opentelemetry-exporter-otlp to backend/pyproject.toml
- Create backend/telemetry.py:
  - configure_tracing(): sets up TracerProvider with OTLP exporter pointing to collector endpoint (OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT from settings)
  - configure_metrics(): sets up MeterProvider with OTLP exporter
  - Instruments FastAPI app with FastAPIInstrumentor
  - Custom span attributes: agent source_id, job count, keyword list hash, scrape duration
- Add OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT and OTEL_SERVICE_NAME to .env.example and settings.py
- Call configure_tracing() and configure_metrics() in main.py lifespan startup
- Instrument each agent run() method with tracer.start_as_current_span(f'{source_id}.run')
- Create docs/OBSERVABILITY.md: architecture diagram of trace flow, span attribute catalogue, metric names

EFFORT: L (8 hours)
PROJECT AREA: Backend
DEPENDENCIES: JD-2-1, JD-3-2, JD-2-2

TESTING CRITERIA:
- Traces appear in OTLP collector on every POST /api/v1/scrape request
- Each agent run() produces a child span under the scrape parent span
- Custom span attributes (source_id, job count) present on agent spans
- Metrics endpoint exposes request count and latency histograms
- docs/OBSERVABILITY.md created with span attribute catalogue

EDGE CASES:
- OTEL collector unavailable: tracing must fail silently and not crash the application; use BatchSpanProcessor with NoOpExporter fallback
- High cardinality span attributes (e.g. job titles) must not be added as span attributes; use span events instead

Summary: Sentry Integration and Frontend Session Replay
Description:
Integrate Sentry for backend error tracking and alerting, and Microsoft Clarity for frontend session replay.

IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS:
- Backend Sentry integration:
  - Add sentry-sdk[fastapi] to backend/pyproject.toml
  - Initialize in main.py lifespan: sentry_sdk.init(dsn=settings.SENTRY_DSN, traces_sample_rate=0.1, environment=settings.APP_ENV)
  - Add SENTRY_DSN to .env.example and settings.py (Optional[str], default None; Sentry disabled if not set)
  - Custom Sentry tags on all captured exceptions: agent source_id, request_id, app_env
  - Alert rules in Sentry (documented in docs/OBSERVABILITY.md): error rate > 1% in 5 minutes triggers PagerDuty/email
- Frontend Microsoft Clarity:
  - Add Clarity script to frontend/app/layout.tsx via next/script with strategy='afterInteractive'
  - CLARITY_PROJECT_ID from NEXT_PUBLIC_CLARITY_PROJECT_ID env var
  - Add NEXT_PUBLIC_CLARITY_PROJECT_ID to .env.example
  - Clarity disabled if env var not set (conditional script injection)
- Update docs/OBSERVABILITY.md: Sentry project setup, alert configuration, Clarity dashboard link

EFFORT: M (5 hours)
PROJECT AREA: Backend + Frontend
DEPENDENCIES: JD-2-1, JD-4-1

TESTING CRITERIA:
- Unhandled exception in FastAPI route captured in Sentry with correct tags
- SENTRY_DSN=None disables Sentry without errors
- Clarity script injected in frontend HTML when NEXT_PUBLIC_CLARITY_PROJECT_ID set
- Clarity script absent from HTML when env var not set
- traces_sample_rate=0.1 confirmed (not 1.0; avoid excessive Sentry transaction volume)

EDGE CASES:
- Sentry must not capture sensitive data (DATABASE_URL, API keys); configure before_send hook to scrub sensitive fields from event payloads
- Clarity session replay must comply with GDPR; document data retention settings and user consent requirements in docs/OBSERVABILITY.md

Implement backend/agents/observability/observability_agent.py to monitor schema conformance rates and trigger hallucination alerts.

IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS:
- Class ObservabilityAgent decorated with @register; source_id = 'observability'
- Responsibilities:
  - Schema conformance monitoring: intercept all LLM responses across ranking, RAG, cover-letter, and question-answer agents; validate response JSON against expected schema; emit conformance_rate metric to Prometheus; alert via Sentry if conformance_rate drops below 99%
  - Hallucination detection: compare LLM-generated content against retrieved source context using cosine similarity; flag responses where similarity < 0.5 as potential hallucinations; emit hallucination_rate metric; alert if hallucination_rate > 1% over rolling 1-hour window
  - Trace enrichment: add schema_valid and hallucination_detected boolean attributes to existing OTLP spans
- async def monitor_response(agent_source_id: str, response: str, schema: dict, context: list[str]) -> ObservabilityResult
- ObservabilityResult: { schema_valid: bool; hallucination_detected: bool; conformance_score: float; similarity_score: float }
- Create backend/agents/observability/AGENT.md

EFFORT: L (10 hours)
PROJECT AREA: Backend
DEPENDENCIES: JD-13-1, JD-13-3, JD-9-2, JD-3-2

TESTING CRITERIA:
- Schema conformance check correctly identifies malformed JSON responses
- Prometheus conformance_rate metric updates after each monitored response
- Sentry alert fires when conformance_rate < 99% (testable with mocked Sentry client)
- Hallucination detected when LLM response similarity to context < 0.5
- ObservabilityResult fields all populated on every call

EDGE CASES:
- ObservabilityAgent must not add more than 20ms to response path; use async background task for hallucination check
- Schema validation must handle partial responses (streaming); buffer full response before validating

Labels:
Implement Supabase Auth with JWT validation middleware in FastAPI, RBAC enforced via JWT claims, Supabase Row-Level Security policies, OWASP Top 10 hardening with Trivy and Bandit CI scans, and GDPR compliance tooling.

SCOPE:
- Supabase Auth + JWT validation middleware; RBAC at route level via JWT claims
- Row-Level Security: users read and write only their own rows
- OWASP Top 10 hardening: Trivy and Bandit in CI; SSRF allowlist; encrypted secrets via Azure Key Vault
- GDPR compliance: export (PDF/JSON/CSV), deletion across all stores, audit logging

MILESTONE: MVP 3 — Full Twelve-Factor Compliance, Observability & Auth

Description:
Implement JWT validation middleware in FastAPI using Supabase Auth public keys, with RBAC enforced at route level via JWT claims.

IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS:
- Add python-jose[cryptography] to backend/pyproject.toml
- Create backend/auth.py:
  - get_jwks(): fetches Supabase JWKS from SUPABASE_JWKS_URI (cached with 1-hour TTL)
  - verify_token(token: str) -> JWTClaims: validates signature, expiry, audience, and issuer; raises 401 on failure
  - JWTClaims: { sub: str; email: str; role: str; app_metadata: dict }
  - get_current_user(token: Annotated[str, Depends(oauth2_scheme)]) -> JWTClaims: FastAPI dependency
  - require_role(role: str) -> Callable: dependency factory enforcing JWT claims role; raises 403 if role not present
- Apply get_current_user dependency to all non-public routes
- Public routes (no auth): GET /health, GET /api/v1/jobs, POST /api/v1/scrape
- Admin-only routes (require_role('admin')): all /api/v1/admin/* endpoints
- Add SUPABASE_JWKS_URI to .env.example and settings.py
- Update docs/SECURITY.md: JWT flow diagram, role definitions, public vs protected routes

EFFORT: L (8 hours)
PROJECT AREA: Backend
DEPENDENCIES: JD-8-1, JD-10-1, JD-2-2

TESTING CRITERIA:
- Valid JWT grants access to protected routes
- Expired JWT returns 401 with WWW-Authenticate header
- JWT with incorrect audience returns 401
- Non-admin JWT accessing /api/v1/admin/* returns 403
- JWKS cache refreshes after TTL without manual restart

EDGE CASES:
- JWKS endpoint unavailable: serve cached keys if available; return 503 if cache empty
- Token rotation: verify_token must support multiple valid keys from JWKS (key ID matching)
- Clock skew: allow 30-second leeway on expiry validation

Implement Supabase Row-Level Security policies ensuring users can only read and write their own data rows.

IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS:
- Create Alembic migration (0003_rls_policies.py):
  - Enable RLS on all user-scoped tables: ALTER TABLE jobs ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; (same for applications, cover_letters, interview_prep, cv)
  - CREATE POLICY jobs_user_isolation ON jobs USING (user_id = auth.uid())
  - CREATE POLICY applications_user_isolation ON applications USING (user_id = auth.uid())
  - Same pattern for cover_letters, interview_prep, cv tables
  - Scrape_runs and dead_letter_queue: admin-only policy using auth.jwt()->>'role' = 'admin'
- Add user_id (UUID) column to all user-scoped tables via Alembic migration (0002_add_user_id.py)
- Update all asyncpg queries to pass authenticated user_id; remove any SELECT * patterns
- Service role bypass: backend uses Supabase service role key (SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY) for admin operations that bypass RLS; add to .env.example
- Document RLS policies in docs/SECURITY.md

EFFORT: L (8 hours)
PROJECT AREA: Backend
DEPENDENCIES: JD-14-1, JD-8-3

TESTING CRITERIA:
- User A cannot read User B rows via GET /api/v1/jobs (returns empty list, not 403)
- User A cannot write to User B rows via POST endpoints (returns 0 rows affected)
- Admin service role key bypasses RLS successfully for DLQ and scrape_runs access
- Alembic migrations 0002 and 0003 run and reverse cleanly
- All user-scoped tables have RLS enabled (query pg_tables and pg_policies to verify)

EDGE CASES:
- RLS policies evaluated per-row; large tables with missing user_id column will fail policy silently; ensure user_id NOT NULL constraint added in migration 0002
- Service role key must never be exposed to frontend; backend-only env var

Implement OWASP Top 10 hardening measures including Trivy and Bandit security scans in CI, SSRF allowlist, and encrypted secrets via Azure Key Vault.

IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS:
- CI security scanning (add to .github/workflows/ci.yml):
  - Trivy scan job: aquasecurity/trivy-action scanning Docker image for HIGH and CRITICAL CVEs; fails CI on CRITICAL findings
  - Bandit scan job: uv run bandit -r backend/ -ll; fails CI on medium or high severity findings
  - Run on every PR and push to main
- SSRF allowlist (add to SecurityMiddleware in backend/agents/security/security_agent.py):
  - Validate all outbound HTTP URLs in scraper agents against allowlist: [linkedin.com, jobserve.com, api.openai.com, api.anthropic.com]
  - Reject requests to private IP ranges (10.x, 172.16-31.x, 192.168.x, 127.x, 169.254.x) with 400
  - Log blocked SSRF attempts to Sentry
- Azure Key Vault integration (update Terraform in JD-11-1):
  - All secrets (DATABASE_URL, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, SENTRY_DSN) stored in Key Vault
  - Container App reads secrets via managed identity; no plaintext env vars in Terraform
- Update docs/SECURITY.md: OWASP mapping table (Top 10 item to mitigation), SSRF allowlist, Key Vault rotation process

EFFORT: L (10 hours)
PROJECT AREA: Backend + Infrastructure
DEPENDENCIES: JD-10-1, JD-11-1, JD-5-4

TESTING CRITERIA:
- Trivy scan job triggers on PR and fails on CRITICAL CVE in Docker image
- Bandit scan job fails on hardcoded secret detected in backend/ source
- SSRF request to 192.168.1.1 returns 400 and logs to Sentry
- SSRF request to linkedin.com allowed through allowlist

EDGE CASES:
- Trivy may report false positives in base image; configure .trivyignore for accepted risks with documented rationale
- Bandit B101 (assert statements) commonly false-positive in test files; configure bandit to skip tests/ directory

  - Returns 202 Accepted with deletion_request_id; status queryable via GET /api/v1/user/deletion-status/{id}

  - Create audit_log table: id, user_id, action (export/delete/login/scrape), timestamp, ip_address, metadata JSONB
  - Log all data access and mutation events via async background task
  - Audit log retention: 90 days; automated cleanup via pg_cron or scheduled Temporal workflow
- Update docs/SECURITY.md: GDPR data map, deletion SLA (72 hours), audit log retention policy

PROJECT AREA: Backend
DEPENDENCIES: JD-14-2, JD-10-2, JD-8-3

TESTING CRITERIA:
- GET /api/v1/user/export?format=json returns all user data as valid JSON file download

- DELETE /api/v1/user/account returns 202 and initiates deletion workflow

Complete Twelve-Factor App compliance across all platform components, implement graceful shutdown for all long-running processes, finalise all admin tooling, and validate disaster recovery procedures with defined RTO and RPO targets.

SCOPE:
- Twelve-Factor audit: verify all 12 factors against production config; document and close gaps
- Graceful shutdown: SIGTERM handlers in uvicorn, Temporal worker, and Playwright sessions
- Admin tooling: replay_dlq.py and run_evals.py fully wired; schedule pause/resume routes
- Disaster recovery: backup restore drill; PostgreSQL WAL + Redis AOF; RTO <= 1 hour, RPO <= 15 minutes

MILESTONE: MVP 3 — Full Twelve-Factor Compliance, Observability & Auth

Audit the platform against all 12 Twelve-Factor App principles, document any gaps, and implement remediation for each gap found.

IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS:
- Create docs/TWELVE-FACTOR-AUDIT.md with a table: Factor | Status | Evidence | Gap | Remediation
- Audit each factor:
  1. Codebase: single repo, multiple deploys via environment variables
  2. Dependencies: pyproject.toml and package.json; no system-level dependencies assumed
  3. Config: all config via environment variables; no config in code
  4. Backing services: DATABASE_URL, OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT treated as attached resources
  5. Build/release/run: Docker image = build; env vars injected at run; Terraform = release
  6. Processes: stateless FastAPI; no local file system state (all state in Supabase)
  7. Port binding: FastAPI on 8000; Nginx on 80; no hardcoded port in application code
  8. Concurrency: horizontal scaling via Container App replicas; Temporal workers scale independently
  9. Disposability: fast startup (< 10s); graceful shutdown (SIGTERM handlers per JD-15-2)
  10. Dev/prod parity: Docker image identical across dev/staging/prod; only env vars differ
  11. Logs: all logs to stdout as structured JSON; no log files written to disk
  12. Admin processes: admin scripts in backend/admin/; run as one-off Temporal workflows or direct CLI
- For each gap found: create a linked sub-task and track to completion before MVP 3 release

EFFORT: M (6 hours)
PROJECT AREA: Documentation + Cross-cutting
DEPENDENCIES: JD-2-3, JD-8-2, JD-13-3

TESTING CRITERIA:
- docs/TWELVE-FACTOR-AUDIT.md exists with all 12 factors audited
- Status column contains PASS, PARTIAL, or FAIL for each factor
- All FAIL and PARTIAL factors have documented remediation tasks
- No application config stored in source code (grep for hardcoded URLs, credentials in backend/)

EDGE CASES:
- Factor 6 (Processes): Playwright scraper sessions are stateful; document as accepted exception with rationale
- Factor 9 (Disposability): Temporal worker checkpoint on SIGTERM must be verified in load test

Implement SIGTERM handlers for uvicorn, Temporal worker, and Playwright scraper sessions to ensure clean shutdown with no data loss.

IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS:
- FastAPI uvicorn graceful shutdown:
  - Add SIGTERM handler in main.py lifespan shutdown block
  - Drain in-flight requests: wait up to 30s for active requests to complete before shutdown
  - Close asyncpg connection pool: await pool.close() in shutdown block
  - Log shutdown sequence steps via get_logger('shutdown')
- Temporal worker graceful shutdown:
  - Register SIGTERM handler in temporal worker process
  - Call worker.shutdown() on SIGTERM; waits for in-progress workflow activities to checkpoint
  - Timeout: 60s; force shutdown after timeout with warning log
- Playwright session cleanup:
  - Wrap all Playwright browser.launch() calls in try/finally; call await browser.close() in finally
  - Register atexit handler in each scraper agent to close browser on process exit
  - Playwright sessions logged as closed in structured log on shutdown
- Update supervisord.conf: stopwaitsecs=90 to allow full drain before supervisor force-kills

EFFORT: M (6 hours)
PROJECT AREA: Backend + Infrastructure
DEPENDENCIES: JD-2-1, JD-10-2, JD-3-3, JD-3-4, JD-1-3

TESTING CRITERIA:
- docker stop sends SIGTERM; application logs graceful shutdown sequence
- In-flight requests complete before process exits (test with slow endpoint + docker stop)
- asyncpg pool closed without error on shutdown
- Playwright browser.close() logged on every scraper agent shutdown
- Temporal worker logs checkpoint on SIGTERM before exiting

EDGE CASES:
- SIGKILL after stopwaitsecs: asyncpg connections may leak in Supabase; document connection cleanup on next startup
- Playwright sessions holding browser processes: atexit handler may not fire on SIGKILL; document known limitation

Design and execute a disaster recovery validation drill covering PostgreSQL WAL archiving, Redis AOF persistence, and RTO/RPO target verification.

IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS:
- PostgreSQL WAL archiving (Supabase):
  - Enable continuous WAL archiving in Supabase project settings (point-in-time recovery)
  - Document recovery procedure in docs/DISASTER-RECOVERY.md: restore to specific timestamp, verify row counts
  - Terraform: set backup_retention_days = 7 on Supabase project (if configurable via provider)
- Redis AOF persistence (if Redis added for caching):
  - Configure appendonly yes in Redis config
  - appendfsync everysec (balance between durability and performance)
  - Document Redis AOF recovery procedure in docs/DISASTER-RECOVERY.md
- DR drill procedure (documented and executed):
  1. Take Supabase backup snapshot
  2. Drop jobs table to simulate data loss
  3. Restore from WAL archive to point before drop
  4. Verify row count matches pre-loss count
  5. Record RTO (time from failure detection to full restoration)
  6. Record RPO (age of oldest data lost)
- RTO target: <= 1 hour; RPO target: <= 15 minutes
- Create docs/DISASTER-RECOVERY.md: runbook, RTO/RPO targets, backup schedule, DR drill results table, escalation contacts (placeholder)

EFFORT: L (8 hours)
PROJECT AREA: Infrastructure
DEPENDENCIES: JD-8-3, JD-11-1

TESTING CRITERIA:
- docs/DISASTER-RECOVERY.md exists with full runbook and DR drill results
- Supabase WAL archiving enabled and confirmed via Supabase dashboard
- DR drill executed and RTO recorded as <= 1 hour
- DR drill executed and RPO recorded as <= 15 minutes
- Row count after restore matches row count before simulated data loss

EDGE CASES:
- Supabase free tier may not support PITR; document tier requirement and upgrade path in docs/DISASTER-RECOVERY.md
- DR drill must be run in staging environment, not production; document environment isolation requirement
- Redis AOF file size growth: document AOF rewrite schedule (auto-aof-rewrite-percentage 100)
